channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: This is how RNG works as an 'equalizer' in Dispatch
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 10, 2026

3 Min Read

Dispatch, the superhero workplace comedy from AdHoc Studio, is lying to you (but for good reason). 

The episodic title has players attempting to save the city by directing a team of dysfunctional supers from crisis to crisis. Pick the right hero for the job, and you'll have a better chance of success. Choose the wrong candidate, and well, those odds plummet. 

The game tells players how likely some of their decisions are to succeed by offering them a percentile readout. One hero might have an 85 percent chance of completing an action. The odds of success for another hero might only be 15 percent. . 

During a talk at GDC Festival of Gaming, AdHoc creative directors and co-founders Nick Herman and Dennis Lenard explain how they designed that RNG mechanic to ensure it met the expectations of players.

"Let's talk about RNG as an equalizer. The initial design scenarios had invisible pass-fail thresholds where players would have to guess what the stat requirements might be. We eventually switched that binary system over to be driven by chance," says Lenart. 

Related:Don Daglow and Rebecca Ann Heineman to be honored at 2026 Game Developers Choice Awards

"For casual players, that means as long as you send someone—even if they're a bad choice—you're going to have even the smallest chance of succeeding. For more experienced gamers, it was an opportunity to min-max and send the perfect team without over or under committing your resources."

Herman accepts that RNG can be divisive, but explains that because Dispatch is a story driven title it seemed like a good fit. There would, for instance, be little value in a hard fail scenario that repeatedly stopped players in their tracks. Even so, he explains it was important to mitigate frustrating experiences—such as missing a 99 percenter. 

"As any hardcore XCOM player knows, one of the tricks Firaxis implemented was to secretly boost the numbers behind the scenes so that it felt fair even it it was unearned. Those guys are pretty smart so we thought we'd do the same," says Herman. 

"After lots of user testing we landed on anything that had over a 76 percent success rate would automatically succeed. After the player benefited there times from this boost we would remove the auto-win and give them true odds again. As soon as they failed above 76 percent, we enabled the three auto-wins again to guarantee they didn't have a string of bad luck and complain the game wasn't fair."

In addition, Lenart explains that any percentage between 1 percent and 14 percent was bumped up to a flat 15 percent chance of success. With those systems in place, players told AdHoc that Dispatch seemed fair—if perhaps a touch easy at times. That safety net, however, was punted out of the window during the final act.

Related:Blue Prince's somber tone was inspired by Myst, says creator

"The final change we made was in the last episode. With things on fire and your dispatching skills being put to the test, we disabled all of these invisible helpers," says Lenart. "For the first time in the entire season the training wheels are off, and as a result the game feels a lot harder—which is exactly what we wanted for our finale."

Game Developer and GDC Festival of Gaming are sibling companies under Informa Festivals.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/this-is-how-the-rng-works-as-an-equalizer-in-dispatch
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Peak co-developer Aggro Crab shares lessons in friendslop
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 10, 2026

5 Min Read

Nick Kaman, studio head of Aggro Crab— the developer you might recognize as one half of the team that made impossibly popular climber Peak—believes there are a few golden rules to follow if you want to hop aboard the friendslop freight train. 

Taking to the stage at GDC Festival of Gaming to discuss the making of the madcap title from Aggro Crab and Landfall Games, Kaman suggests a core factor in Peak's success was the team's determination to create a "social-first game" that hinges on cooperation and communication. 

"First and foremost—and I mean you can play alone—but the feeling you get when you get to the peak, and you only did it because your friends helped you along the way, is a pretty beautiful feeling," says Kaman. "That's why you can't get items out of your backpack on your own. You need a friend to do it. It's just one more layer of friction that means you have to interact socially." 

Related:Indie fund Outersloth has rejected every generative AI pitch it has received

Carrying that thread, Kaman explains the team had a hunch that if a player was too good they would simply abandon their friends and head for the summit alone. That's why they implemented a guidebook that can only be read by one person who must then orate the rules to their companions (hopefully telling the truth, but perhaps sprinkling in a few white lies). 

Notably, that guidebook also contains a fundamental Rule Zero: 'Never abandon a friend in need.' 

"People just listen to this and role play out as their character, because that's the kind of game it is. And they would tell their friends 'Never abandon a friend in need, remember rule zero!' and it kind of just works," says Kaman. 

There is, of course, a safeguard. If the game detects someone has disregarded rule zero, there is a five percent chance a zombie scoutmaster will come and murder them. Good times. 

Kaman also believes that first-person cameras are integral to friendslop experiences, because they push players to really embody their characters and roleplay. It also, crucially, enhances the impact of proximity chat—with eyes following players and mouths animating with speech.

To hammer home that point, Kaman plays a viral clip dubbed 'THE SCREAM'. Please only continue reading once you have clicked that link. I beg you. 

It's a harrowing incident but one that perfectly displays the need for communication in Peak (and the hilarity and sorrow that ensues when players become too complacent). 

"You have to talk about your best way up the mountain. This was a natural evolution of the design. You need to look around and see which path looked easier, discuss it, and agree on something to move up," says Kaman. 

Related:'If we love this work, we have to protect it:' Heart Machine workers secure voluntary union recognition

"Knowledge is passed down from player to player, such as which mushroom is poisonous. We want all of that to be talked about and passed down that way. Then we made it so that you could be a ghost when you're dead. This is a departure from other friendslop games, where once you're dead you are out of the voice comms—which makes sense because a lot of those games are horror based and want to make it scary. 

"But here, the ghost actually gets to keep interacting with people. You can talk to your friends and you have this bird's eye view of the situation so you can be uniquely helpful." 

Devs often talk about how design constraints can birth greatness. Kaman explains that in the case of Peak, it actually brought Aggro Crab and Landfall closer to the almighty. Allow them to explain. 

"The daily map was a big deal [in engaging players], but this started as a design constraint as we actually couldn't generate these maps on the player's end at runtime the way you would with and procedural generation of roguelike game. So, what we do is bake all the maps into Unity Editor fourteen at a time and then every two weeks we have to patch the game to update the maps. We still have to do this," he says. 

Related:Scopely hits $15 billion in lifetime revenue

"We really didn't have a lot of foresight here, but the players loved it. It's unique to the game and it means you get multiple chances at the same mountain. Maybe you've tried and failed but now you understand the route so you can try again that day. Then, you have a new challenge tomorrow. It also became a source of community because you can go on the subreddit or Discord and say 'the map is fucking bullshit today, right guys?' and have that shared experience of what the mountain is. 

"Oh, and by the way, people always wonder how we generate the mountain and design it to be climbable. We don't. We throw rocks at the wall and pray, just like in real life. That's what God does, and people climb it. We found that was way more compelling than any level design we do by hand." 

P.S: If you're wondering what the technical constraint is that forced the team to handle maps in that way, here's an explainer from a slide that flew by at breakneck speed during Kaman's talk: 

  • It's because to generate the map you need an RTX-compatible graphics card to bake out something called an occlusion map that generates a 3D volume over the play space indicating which areas are in the open and which are cavernous. This is useful for determining voice reverberation (caves), whether or not the blizzard hits you, spawning of plants vs mushrooms, and general baked lighting. Since we can't guarantee the player has a good graphics card we have to bake maps in editor. 

Game Developer and GDC Festival of Gaming are sibling companies under Informa Festivals.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/peak-co-developer-aggro-crab-shares-lessons-in-friendslop
